caveats: a: The 2023 precision-adoption data are for broad crop-farm size groups, not NAICS 111940.
a: The hay-specific sensing source is older research evidence and reports limited commercial implementation at that time.
rho: Farm size strongly predicts technology uptake, but the LMM population's acreage and machinery age are not observed.
rho: Weather and crop-condition variability make successful demonstrations an incomplete guide to commercial implementation.
e: The frozen n is missing and must not be replaced with broader Census farm counts.
e: USDA's 95% family-farm share covers all agriculture, not eligible LMM hay farms.
s5: TOTAL measures acres and transfer methods, not closed business-control transactions.
s5: Trusts, wills, gifts, leases, and gradual family succession may not qualify.
q: The cited market report covers one drought-affected state and week rather than national LMM economics.
q: No source directly observes AI-benefit sharing in hay sales.
d5: National production includes hay grown for captive livestock use outside the lens.
d5: Drought can simultaneously reduce supply, increase supplemental feeding, cause herd liquidation, and move prices, making tons and receipts diverge.
o: The estimate separates operator necessity from employee labor intensity.
o: Feed substitution affects demand quantity in d5; it should not be double counted as software displacement.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring seasonal and annual forage sales by ton or bale, differentiated by species, cutting, moisture, protein, digestibility, bale form, certification, location, and delivery; transactions range from repeat direct relationships to brokered or spot sales.
lens.excluded_activities: Livestock farms growing forage mainly for captive use, grain-hay producers classified with grains, grass or hay seed farms, hobby farms, passive farmland landlords, custom hay contractors without crop ownership, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and farms outside the roughly $1-10M normalized EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market independent commercial farms primarily growing hay, alfalfa, clover, or mixed hay and repeatedly selling baled, chopped, or otherwise harvested forage to external dairy, beef, equine, feed, export, or other customers.

a.bridge: USDA measures precision-technology adoption across broad crop farms and documents hay-specific sensing research, not the wage-weighted share of current not-yet-automated tasks in LMM hay farms. I translate guidance, machine control, yield and moisture sensing, imagery, irrigation optimization, weather-based harvest planning, predictive maintenance, marketing, and administration into task exposure, while discounting because cutting, conditioning, raking, baling, stacking, irrigation repair, field intervention, and quality release remain physical and because hay yield-mapping commercialization has lagged. Broad crop-farm adoption may overstate exposure where hay farms use older equipment.
caveats: The 2023 precision-adoption data are for broad crop-farm size groups, not NAICS 111940.
The hay-specific sensing source is older research evidence and reports limited commercial implementation at that time.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task study across irrigated alfalfa, dryland grass, and mixed-hay LMM farms, linked to measured labor changes from autonomous equipment, moisture and quality sensors, imagery, irrigation control, and office AI, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: Hay production is highly mechanized and follows repeat field operations, but harvest timing, moisture, weather, bale handling, and multiple cuttings require judgment and physical intervention. AI can reduce supervision and information work and enable higher autonomy, yet already-installed mechanization and guidance must not be counted as new exposure.

d5.bridge: NASS measures recent national hay production and livestock inventories, while ERS projects the broader cattle cycle; none is a five-year constant-quality demand forecast for externally sold NAICS 111940 hay. I start from 2025 all-hay production of 123.0 million tons, up 0.5%, and infer modest five-year demand from an eventual cattle-herd recovery and stable-to-higher dairy cows, while discounting because a substantial share of forage is captive, pasture conditions and drought change use, and feed substitution can break the link. The cattle projection may overstate commercial hay demand if recovered forage comes from owned pasture or silage.
caveats: National production includes hay grown for captive livestock use outside the lens.
Drought can simultaneously reduce supply, increase supplemental feeding, cause herd liquidation, and move prices, making tons and receipts diverge.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year balance sheet for commercial hay by type and region, separating captive use from sales and linking livestock inventories, pasture conditions, feed substitution, exports, and stocks, would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: Hay is a recurring biological input for dairy, beef, equine, and other animals. National production was nearly flat in 2025, current beef cow inventory is low, and longer-term cattle projections imply some recovery, while dairy cow numbers are stable to higher. Population, exports, drought, pasture quality, and ration economics create regional swings but support only modest central growth.

caveats: The frozen n is missing and must not be replaced with broader Census farm counts.
USDA's 95% family-farm share covers all agriculture, not eligible LMM hay farms.
e.change_evidence: A farm-level dataset combining NAICS, forage sales versus captive use, ownership, related livestock entities, acres, gross cash farm income, normalized EBITDA, and operator status would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: A farm classified primarily in hay farming is more likely than a livestock farm to sell forage externally and fit the repeat-revenue model. Eligibility is reduced for captive-use forage, hobby or lifestyle farms, passive landowners, custom operators without crop ownership, seasonal or marginal operations, and farms outside the EBITDA band. With frozen n missing, this share does not establish how many acquisition candidates exist.

l.detail: No receipts concept for this code (government / SUSB out of scope). QCEW government wages exist ($1,232,345) but there is no revenue denominator; labor_share left null.
l.rationale: dataset labor_share has no defensible value

n.detail: margin 0.0804 (margins.json[11]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Farming/Agriculture EBITDA/Sales 8.04%); revenue/employee $299,458 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 11); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: The estimate separates operator necessity from employee labor intensity.
Feed substitution affects demand quantity in d5; it should not be double counted as software displacement.
o.change_evidence: Evidence on fully autonomous managed-hay acres, livestock-customer insourcing, vertical integration, and contractual responsibility under equipment- or farming-as-a-service models would materially change the operator-required share.
o.rationale: Nearly all year-five demand for hay still requires an accountable farm operator to secure land and water, establish stands, manage fertility and pests, time repeated cuttings, control moisture and quality, maintain equipment, store product, and bear weather and customer risk. AI and autonomous machinery can reduce labor but do not eliminate the operating enterprise; small displacement could come from vertically integrated livestock farms, managed-farming platforms, or alternative feeds.

q.bridge: USDA market reports observe localized hay trades, quality descriptions, drought-sensitive availability, and prices, not the five-year share of implemented AI benefits retained by LMM hay farms. I translate bulky freight economics, perishable harvest timing, quality differentiation, and repeat local customer relationships into moderate retention, while allowing competitive bids, alternative forage, buyer concentration, and land or equipment costs to absorb savings. A drought-affected Colorado report can overstate pricing power in normal or surplus regions.
caveats: The cited market report covers one drought-affected state and week rather than national LMM economics.
No source directly observes AI-benefit sharing in hay sales.
q.change_evidence: Customer-level margin bridges after productivity projects, repeat-contract terms, delivered-mile economics, quality premiums, rejected-load data, regional bid histories, and cash-rent and equipment-cost resets would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Hay is less fungible than exchange-traded grain because delivered distance, moisture, protein, cutting, bale form, and reliability matter. Those frictions support retention for consistent quality and proximity, but dairy and feed buyers remain cost-sensitive and productivity diffusion can reduce regional prices or raise rents and equipment charges.

caveats: Farm size strongly predicts technology uptake, but the LMM population's acreage and machinery age are not observed.
Weather and crop-condition variability make successful demonstrations an incomplete guide to commercial implementation.
rho.change_evidence: A five-year LMM hay-farm cohort reporting acres and operations automated, installed cost, interventions, downtime, moisture and quality errors, labor hours, crop losses, insurance constraints, and payback would materially narrow the range.
rho.rationale: Guidance, imagery, moisture and quality sensing, irrigation decision support, maintenance triage, scheduling, marketing, and recordkeeping can be implemented within five years. Driver-out field and bale-handling autonomy faces equipment replacement cycles, interoperability, dust and debris, irregular fields, road movement, rural connectivity, insurance and safety, and high losses when rain or equipment failure interrupts a narrow harvest window.

s5.bridge: USDA's 2024 TOTAL survey measures expected ownership transfer of farmland acres across all agriculture, not qualifying control transfers of NAICS 111940 operating businesses, while the Census reports producer age and family ownership broadly. I use about 5% of farmland slated for sale or gift within five years as a low anchor and raise the firm-level center because the average producer is 58.1 and operator control can change without all land transferring. The evidence can overstate actionable deals when acres pass within families or trusts and understate operating transitions on rented land.
caveats: TOTAL measures acres and transfer methods, not closed business-control transactions.
Trusts, wills, gifts, leases, and gradual family succession may not qualify.
s5.change_evidence: Commodity- and size-specific operator-control transition data plus interviews with farm lenders, brokers, attorneys, and owners of commercial hay farms would materially change the probability.
s5.rationale: Aging family ownership creates turnover, but hay farms can be transferred piecemeal as land, machinery, leases, and customer relationships rather than as an intact control-ready company. Internal succession and leasing are common alternatives to third-party sale, supporting only a 15% central qualifying-transfer probability.

principal_driver: Localized freight economics and quality-sensitive repeat demand can protect productivity gains while AI improves timing, sensing, equipment utilization, and labor efficiency.
principal_weakness: Missing labor and firm-count denominators, plus fragmented family and asset transfers, prevent a reliable estimate of actionable acquisition capacity.

sources.supports: Official scope covering farms primarily growing hay, alfalfa, clover, or mixed hay and excluding grain hay and grass or hay seed.
sources.title: NAICS Sector 11 Definitions: 111940 Hay Farming

sources.supports: Broad crop-farm adoption proxy: in 2023 guidance autosteering was used by 52% of midsize and 70% of large-scale crop farms, with adoption motivated by yield, labor time, input cost, fatigue, and environmental benefits.
sources.title: Precision agriculture use increases with farm size and varies widely by technology

sources.supports: Hay-specific technology proxy: yield and moisture sensing had been developed for windrowers, forage harvesters, and balers, but commercial adoption was constrained by cost, uncertain payback, and moisture-measurement difficulty.
sources.title: Technology background and best practices: yield mapping in hay and forage

sources.supports: National quantity evidence: 49.557 million hay acres yielded 123.031 million tons in 2025, with production up 0.5% from 2024.
sources.title: USDA NASS 2025 Annual Crop Production

sources.supports: Near-term forage-demand proxy: 86.2 million cattle and calves as of January 1, 2026; beef cows were down 1% while milk cows increased to 9.57 million.
sources.title: United States cattle inventory down slightly

sources.supports: Farm-structure proxy: 95% of US farms were family-owned and operated and the average producer age was 58.1.
sources.title: USDA releases 2022 Census of Agriculture data

sources.supports: Transfer proxy: about 43 million acres, roughly 5% of US farmland, are slated for ownership transfer by sale or gift within five years, including 23 million acres expected to sell to nonrelatives.
sources.title: Most of the U.S. Rented Farmland is Owned by Non-Farmers

sources.supports: Localized commercial-structure proxy showing direct hay trade by type, bale, quality, use, and price, with demand and availability affected by drought and water constraints.
sources.title: USDA AMS Colorado Direct Hay Report for week ending April 24, 2026

sources.supports: Longer-term livestock proxy: cattle inventory is projected to recover from a multidecade low through 2033, while cattle production and prices remain cyclical.
sources.title: Livestock Production Cycles Affect Long-Term Price Outlook for Cattle, Hogs, and Chickens

title: Hay Farming
